description: The APOE ε4 allele is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease. Carriers have 3-8x increased risk.

diet: Mediterranean diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids
Reduce saturated fats and red meat
Increase consumption of leafy greens and berries
Limit alcohol consumption
exercise: Aerobic exercise 150+ minutes/week
Strength training 2x/week to improve insulin sensitivity
Yoga and mindfulness to reduce cortisol
lifestyle: Prioritize 7-9 hours of quality sleep
Mental stimulation through puzzles and learning
Stress management practices

description: APOE variant affecting lipid metabolism and cardiovascular health. Associated with altered LDL cholesterol processing.

diet: Low-cholesterol diet
Increase fiber intake from oats and legumes
Omega-3 rich fish 2-3 times per week
exercise: Regular cardio 30 min/day
Maintain healthy BMI through active lifestyle
lifestyle: Avoid smoking
Moderate alcohol only

description: MTHFR C677T variant reduces enzyme activity by 30-70%, affecting folate metabolism and homocysteine levels. Linked to elevated heart disease and stroke risk.

diet: Increase folate from leafy greens, lentils, and fortified foods
Take methylfolate (not folic acid) supplements
Reduce methionine-heavy foods (red meat)
B12-rich foods: eggs, dairy, salmon
exercise: Moderate-intensity exercise to lower homocysteine
Avoid extreme endurance training without medical supervision
lifestyle: Avoid alcohol which depletes folate
Manage stress to reduce inflammation

description: MTHFR A1298C variant mildly reduces enzyme function. Often combined with rs1801133 for compounded effects.

diet: Folate-rich foods
B-vitamin complex supplementation
Adequate hydration
exercise: Regular moderate exercise
lifestyle: Prenatal vitamins with methylfolate if planning pregnancy

description: FTO gene variant strongly associated with increased body mass index and obesity predisposition. AA homozygotes have ~3kg higher BMI on average.

diet: High-protein, low-glycemic diet
Avoid processed foods and added sugars
Intermittent fasting may be beneficial
Portion control strategies
exercise: High-intensity interval training (HIIT) is especially effective
Daily step count target of 10,000+
Resistance training to build muscle mass
lifestyle: Sleep 7-8 hours (poor sleep worsens FTO effects)
Mindful eating practices
Food diary journaling

description: LCT gene variant associated with reduced lactase enzyme persistence into adulthood, causing difficulty digesting lactose.

diet: Use lactase enzyme supplements with dairy
Opt for lactose-free dairy products
Alternative calcium sources: fortified plant milks, sardines, broccoli
Hard cheeses and yogurt are better tolerated
exercise: Weight-bearing exercise to maintain bone density
screening: DEXA bone density scan if dairy is severely restricted
lifestyle: Gradual dairy introduction to build tolerance
Calcium supplement if dietary intake is insufficient

description: ACTN3 R577X variant affects alpha-actinin-3 in fast-twitch muscle fibers. XX genotype more suited for endurance vs power sports.

diet: Adequate protein 1.6-2.2g/kg bodyweight
Creatine supplementation may be beneficial
exercise: Focus on endurance sports: running, cycling, swimming
Higher rep, moderate weight training
Excellent recovery is key
lifestyle: Prioritize recovery and sleep for optimal muscle adaptation

description: HFE C282Y variant is the primary cause of hereditary hemochromatosis — excess iron accumulation that can damage the liver, heart, and joints.

diet: Reduce red meat consumption
Avoid iron supplements and vitamin C with meals (enhances iron absorption)
Tea and coffee with meals reduces iron absorption
Avoid alcohol which damages iron-loaded liver
exercise: Regular moderate exercise to help utilize stored iron
screening: Annual serum ferritin and transferrin saturation
Consider phlebotomy therapy if ferritin elevated
lifestyle: Do not donate blood without informing about HH status
Consult hematologist proactively

description: CYP2C19 *2 loss-of-function variant affects drug metabolism. Poor metabolizers have reduced clopidogrel effectiveness, increasing cardiovascular event risk.

diet: Avoid grapefruit which inhibits CYP2C19
Maintain liver-friendly diet
exercise: Regular cardio to support cardiovascular health
screening: Pharmacogenomics panel before starting new medications
Inform all doctors of this genetic variant
lifestyle: Carry medical alert information about poor metabolizer status
Always discuss medications with awareness of this variant

diseases: Cardiovascular Risk with High Coffee Intake
description: CYP1A2 variant affecting caffeine metabolism speed. Slow metabolizers have increased cardiovascular risk with high coffee intake.

diet: Limit coffee to 1-2 cups per day
Avoid energy drinks
Switch to green tea for lower caffeine with antioxidants
exercise: Avoid pre-workout caffeine supplements
lifestyle: Monitor caffeine sensitivity symptoms

description: DRD2 Taq1A variant reduces dopamine D2 receptor density, associated with reward deficiency syndrome and addiction vulnerability.

diet: Tyrosine-rich foods to support dopamine: eggs, chicken, almonds
Reduce sugar and processed food dopamine spikes
exercise: Exercise is the best natural dopamine booster
Team sports for social reward stimulation
screening: Addiction risk counseling if substance use is a concern
lifestyle: Mindfulness and meditation
Structured routine to support dopamine regulation
Seek therapy for reward-seeking behaviors

description: APOA2 variant interacts strongly with saturated fat intake to affect BMI. High saturated fat diet significantly worsens outcomes.

diet: Critical: Limit saturated fat < 22g/day
Replace butter with olive oil
Lean proteins over fatty meats
exercise: Combination cardio and strength training
lifestyle: Read food labels carefully for saturated fat content

description: HLA region variant strongly associated with autoimmune conditions including Type 1 Diabetes and Celiac disease risk.

diet: Trial gluten-free diet if digestive issues present
Anti-inflammatory Mediterranean diet
Monitor blood glucose levels
exercise: Regular moderate exercise to improve insulin sensitivity
lifestyle: Reduce environmental triggers: stress, infections
Probiotics for gut microbiome support

description: TOMM40 poly-T variant affects mitochondrial protein import and age of Alzheimer's onset when combined with APOE variants.

diet: Ketogenic or low-carb diet to support brain energy
Antioxidant-rich foods: blueberries, dark chocolate, green tea
exercise: Aerobic exercise shown to slow neurodegeneration
Dance-based exercise for dual motor-cognitive benefit
screening: PET scan baseline if family history present
lifestyle: Social engagement and continued learning
Neuroprotective sleep hygiene

description: APOC3 variant affecting triglyceride clearance. Elevated triglycerides linked to pancreatitis and cardiovascular disease.

diet: Severely limit alcohol
Reduce refined carbohydrates and sugars
Omega-3 rich foods and fish oil supplements
exercise: Regular aerobic exercise lowers triglycerides significantly
screening: Fasting triglyceride panel every 3 months initially
lifestyle: Avoid high-fat meals
Weight management

description: LPL S447X variant affects lipoprotein lipase activity and triglyceride metabolism.

diet: Low fat diet
Complex carbohydrates over simple sugars
Generous vegetable intake
exercise: Consistent aerobic exercise is highly effective for LPL function
lifestyle: Healthy body weight maintenance

description: TNF-α promoter variant increases inflammatory cytokine production, linked to inflammatory and autoimmune diseases.

diet: Strong anti-inflammatory diet
Turmeric/curcumin daily
Ginger, garlic, green tea
Eliminate processed foods
exercise: Anti-inflammatory yoga and walking
Avoid overtraining which increases TNF-α
screening: Rheumatoid factor if joint pain develops
lifestyle: Stress reduction is critical
Quality sleep reduces inflammatory markers
Cold exposure therapy may help

description: SLCO1B1 variant significantly increases risk of muscle pain and myopathy when taking statin cholesterol medications.

diet: Natural cholesterol-lowering foods: oats, nuts, plant sterols
exercise: Regular exercise to manage cholesterol naturally
lifestyle: Inform your doctor about this variant before any statin prescription
Consider alternative cholesterol therapies

description: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor variant affects bronchodilation response and metabolic rate during exercise.

diet: Anti-inflammatory diet for airway health
Avoid food triggers: sulfites, dairy if sensitive
exercise: Warm up slowly to prevent exercise-induced bronchospasm
Swimming is excellent for respiratory fitness
screening: Pulmonary function test if breathing issues
lifestyle: Avoid air pollutants and smoke
Carry rescue inhaler if prescribed

description: COMT Val158Met variant affects dopamine and norepinephrine breakdown. Met/Met variants have slower breakdown — better cognition under low stress, worse under high stress.

diet: Avoid excess catecholamine triggers: coffee, stimulants
Magnesium-rich foods for stress resilience
exercise: Moderate exercise optimal — avoid extreme stress sports
screening: Mental health evaluation if anxiety is significant
lifestyle: Stress management is paramount
Mindfulness meditation shown to be especially effective
Creative outlets for dopamine regulation

description: The rs433456 variant is associated with the SLC22A4 gene, which codes for an organic cation transporter involved in the regulation of various compounds, including xenobiotics and endogenous substances. Variants in this gene have been linked to an increased risk of kidney diseases, such as IgA nephropathy and lupus nephritis, likely due to altered transport function and subsequent inflammation.

diet: Increase your intake of antioxidant-rich foods, such as berries and leafy greens
Choose low-sodium options to reduce blood pressure and kidney strain
Incorporate omega-3 fatty acids from sources like fatty fish and flaxseeds
Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water and limiting sugary drinks
Limit your consumption of processed meats and saturated fats
Consider a Mediterranean-style diet, rich in whole grains, fruits, and vegetables
Consult with a registered dietitian or healthcare professional to create a personalized meal plan
exercise: Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise, such as brisk walking or cycling, per week
Incorporate strength training exercises, like weightlifting or bodyweight exercises, to improve overall health and reduce blood pressure
Engage in flexibility and stretching exercises, such as yoga or Pilates, to improve circulation and reduce stress
Consider high-intensity interval training (HIIT) for improved cardiovascular health and weight management
Consult with a fitness professional or healthcare provider to create a personalized exercise plan
screening: Get your blood pressure checked at least once a year, or more frequently if you have a family history of hypertension
Have your kidney function tested every 2-3 years, or as recommended by your healthcare provider
Schedule regular check-ups with your healthcare provider to monitor your overall health and address any concerns
Consider getting screened for diabetes, as it can increase your risk of kidney disease
Discuss your screening schedule with your healthcare provider to ensure you're getting the necessary tests and check-ups
lifestyle: Maintain a healthy weight to reduce the risk of kidney disease and other conditions
Quit smoking and avoid exposure to secondhand smoke
Limit your alcohol intake to reduce the risk of kidney damage
Manage stress through techniques like meditation, deep breathing, or yoga
Get enough sleep, aiming for 7-8 hours per night, to help regulate your immune system and overall health

description: The rs10549278 variant is a single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) located in the CDKN2A gene, which encodes a tumor suppressor protein. This variation has been associated with an increased risk of developing certain types of cancer, including melanoma and pancreatic cancer. The risk allele T is associated with a moderate increase in cancer risk.

diet: Eat a balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains to help reduce cancer risk
Include cancer-fighting foods like berries, leafy greens, and cruciferous vegetables in your diet
Limit your intake of processed and red meats, which may increase cancer risk
Choose healthy fats like olive oil and avocado oil instead of saturated and trans fats
Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water and limiting sugary drinks
Consider taking a daily multivitamin or supplement after consulting with your healthcare provider
Incorporate cancer-protective spices like turmeric and ginger into your meals
exercise: Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic exercise per week
Incorporate strength-training exercises into your routine at least two times per week
Engage in high-intensity interval training (HIIT) for added cancer-fighting benefits
Try to incorporate physical activity into your daily routine, such as taking the stairs or walking to work
Consider working with a personal trainer or fitness coach to develop a customized exercise plan
screening: Get a skin exam by a dermatologist every 6-12 months to monitor for melanoma
Discuss your risk factors with your healthcare provider to determine the best screening schedule for pancreatic cancer
Follow the recommended screening guidelines for other types of cancer, such as colon, breast, and cervical cancer
Get a yearly physical exam to monitor your overall health and catch any potential issues early
Consider genetic counseling to discuss your risk factors and develop a personalized screening plan
lifestyle: Don't smoke, as tobacco use increases cancer risk
Limit your sun exposure and use protective measures like sunscreen and clothing to reduce skin cancer risk
Maintain a healthy weight through a combination of diet and exercise
Get enough sleep, aiming for 7-9 hours per night, to help regulate stress and reduce cancer risk
Manage stress through techniques like meditation, yoga, or deep breathing exercises

diet: Increase your intake of omega-3 fatty acids, found in fatty fish, flaxseeds, and walnuts, to help reduce inflammation

Incorporate anti-inflammatory herbs and spices, such as turmeric and ginger, into your meals
exercise: Engage in regular physical activity, such as brisk walking or yoga, to help reduce stress and promote overall health
Aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate-intensity exercise per day
Incorporate strength training exercises to help maintain muscle mass and support overall health
Consider high-intensity interval training (HIIT) to improve cardiovascular health and reduce inflammation
Make time for relaxation and stress-reducing activities, such as meditation or deep breathing exercises
screening: Schedule regular check-ups with your healthcare provider to monitor your digestive health
Undergo routine blood tests to check for signs of inflammation or nutrient deficiencies
Consider colon cancer screening, such as a colonoscopy, if you have a family history of the disease
Get tested for celiac disease or other gastrointestinal conditions if you experience persistent symptoms
Discuss your genetic risk with your healthcare provider and develop a personalized screening plan
lifestyle: Manage stress through relaxation techniques, such as meditation or yoga
Get enough sleep, aiming for 7-8 hours per night, to help regulate your immune system
Avoid smoking and limit your exposure to secondhand smoke
